request.question: Who wrote the fantasy novella which was reworked in 2014 with a series of paintings by a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954?

title: La Leocadia
The Wall of Serpents
The Castle of Iron
Gaspar de la Huerta
The Roaring Trumpet
The Ice Dragon
The Green Magician
Luis Royo
The Mathematics of Magic
Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro
sentences: La Leocadia (Spanish: Doña Leocadia) or The Seductress (Spanish: Una Manola) are names given to a mural by the Spanish artist Francisco Goya (1746–1828), completed sometime between 1819–23, as one of his series of 14 "Black Paintings".
It shows a woman commonly identified as Goya's maid, companion and, most likely lover, Leocadia Weiss.
She is dressed in a dark, almost funeral maja dress, and leans against what is either a mantelpiece or burial mound, as she looks outward at the viewer with a sorrowful expression.

"Leocadia" is one of the final of the "Black Paintings", which he painted in his seventies at a time when he was consumed by political, physical and psychological turmoil, after he fled to the country from his position as court painter in Madrid.
The Wall of Serpents is a fantasy novella by American science fiction and fantasy authors L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The fourth story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the June 1953 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Fantasy Fiction".

It first appeared in book form, together with its sequel, "The Green Magician", in the collection "Wall of Serpents", issued in hardcover by Avalon Books in 1960; the book has been reissued by a number of other publishers since.
It has also been reprinted in various anthologies and collections, including "Great Short Novels of Adult Fantasy I" (1972), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Italian and German.
The Castle of Iron is the title of a fantasy novella by American authors L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt, and of the novel into which it was later expanded by the same authors.
It was the third story (and afterwards the second volume) in their Harold Shea series.
As a 35,000 word novella it was first published in the fantasy magazine "Unknown" for April, 1941.
The revised and expanded novel version was first published in hardcover by Gnome Press in 1950, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1962.
The book has been reprinted by a number of other publishers since its first appearance.
An E-book edition was published by Gollancz's SF Gateway imprint on September 29, 2011 as part of a general release of de Camp's works in electronic form.
The novel has been combined with other books in the series in the omnibus editions "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989) and "" (2007).
It has also been translated into Italian.
Gaspar de la Huerta (1645–1714) was a Spanish artist born at Campillo de Altobuey in Cuenca.

At an early age, seeking instruction in Valencia, he fell into the hands of Jesualda Sanchez, the bustling widow of Pedro Infant, a third-rate painter, who continued on her own account her husband's school for the manufacture of religious pictures.
La Huerta, nevertheless, attained some skill as a draughtsman and colourist, and married the widow's well-dowered daughter.
Working for moderate prices he found abundant employment in the neighbouring churches and convents.
For the Franciscans he painted the "Jubilee of the Porciuncula", and for the Dominicans the picture which long served as a veil to the wondrous image of "Our Lady of the Forsaken".
He died at Valencia in 1714.
The Museum of Valencia possesses a pleasing picture of "Christ and the Virgin enthroned", the latter with the bright complexion peculiar to Valencian beauty.
"The Roaring Trumpet" is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The initial story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the May 1940 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Unknown".

It first appeared in book form, together with its sequel, "The Mathematics of Magic", in the collection "The Incomplete Enchanter", issued in hardcover by Henry Holt and Company in 1941, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1960.

It has since been reprinted in various collections by numerous other publishers, including "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Incompleat Enchanter" (1979), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Dutch and Italian.
In 2016, the story was shortlisted for the Retro Hugo Award for Best Novella.
The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".
It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".
The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.
The Green Magician is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The fifth story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the November 1954 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Beyond Fiction".

It first appeared in book form, together with "The Wall of Serpents", in the collection "Wall of Serpents", issued in hardcover by Avalon Books in 1960; the book has been reissued by a number of other publishers since.

It has also been reprinted in various magazines, anthologies and collections, including "The Dragon" (June–July 1978), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), "Masterpieces of Fantasy and Enchantment" (1988), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Italian and German.
Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.

He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.
"The Mathematics of Magic" is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt, the second story in their Harold Shea series.
It was first published in the August 1940 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Unknown".

It first appeared in book form, together with the preceding novella, "The Roaring Trumpet", in the collection "The Incomplete Enchanter", issued in hardcover by Henry Holt and Company in 1941, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1960.

It has since been reprinted in various collections by numerous other publishers, including "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Incompleat Enchanter" (1979), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Dutch and Italian.
In 2016, the story was shortlisted for the Retro Hugo Award for Best Novella.

Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro or The Holy Office (Spanish: Peregrinación a la fuente de San Isidro or El Santo Official ) are names given to an oil mural by the Spanish artist Francisco Goya (1746–1828), probably completed between 1821 and 1823.
The mural is one of the fourteen Black Paintings that Goya applied in oil on the plaster walls of his house.
Between 1874 and 1878 the paintings were transferred to canvas supports under the direction of the art restorer of the Museo del Prado, Salvador Martinez Cubells.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the fantasy novella which was reworked in 2014 with a series of paintings by a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954?
hop1_docs: The Ice Dragon | The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".  It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".  The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.

Luis Royo | Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.  He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.

Les Femmes d'Alger | Les Femmes d'Alger (Women of Algiers) is a series of 15 paintings and numerous drawings by the Spanish cubist artist Pablo Picasso.  The series, created in 1954-1955, was inspired by Eugène Delacroix's 1834 painting "The Women of Algiers in their Apartment" (French: "Femmes d'Alger dans leur appartement" ).  The series is one of several painted by Picasso in tribute to artists that he admired.
Jesús Olalla | Jesús "Josu" Olalla Iraeta (born July 15, 1971) is a Spanish handball player who competed in the 1996 Summer Olympics and in the 2000 Summer Olympics.

Chris Martin (artist) | Chris Martin (born 1954) is an American artist based in Brooklyn, New York.  Martin was born in Washington DC.  He makes abstract paintings with elements of collage which reference New Age and nature imagery.  Martin is influenced by artists such as Paul Feeley, Yayoi Kusama, Alfred Jensen and others who are also concerned with the formal qualities of painting.

Sarah Dollard | Sarah Dollard (born 21 January 1980) is an Australian screenwriter, living and working in the United Kingdom.  She made her start in writing for television on the long-running Australian soap opera "Neighbours", before moving to the UK in 2008.  Stints working in the script departments of British fantasy and science fiction TV shows, "Merlin" and "Primeval", led to writing episodes of BBC Three supernatural television series "Being Human" and British spy thriller "The Game", which first broadcast on BBC America.  She created and wrote the Welsh romantic comedy series "Cara Fi" (Love Me), which debuted on S4C in 2014.  She wrote the episode "Face the Raven" for Series 9 and the episode "Thin Ice" for Series 10 of Doctor Who.

The Copper Promise | The Copper Promise is a 2013 fantasy novella series by Jen Williams and her debut work.  It was initially released between December 2013 and January 2014 as a set of four e-book novellas and has since been published in paperback format as a single volume through Headline (ISBN  ).
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- The Ice Dragon — [1]: "The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin,"
- reworked in 2014 — [1]: "reworked in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo."
- Luis Royo — [2]: "Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla,"
- Chris Martin (born 1954) — [5]: "Chris Martin (born 1954) is an American artist"

Ambiguities:
- "born 1954" appears for both "Luis Royo (born 1954)" [2] and "Chris Martin (born 1954)" [5]; only Luis Royo is described as "born in Olalla."

Bridge / missing fact:
- None — the passages explicitly connect the 2014 reworking by Luis Royo to the novella and name its author.

Retrieval direction:
- Optional verification pages: "The Ice Dragon" (Wikipedia) and "Luis Royo" (Wikipedia).
- Final conclusion: The novella was written by George R. R. Martin.

It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".  The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.

Luis Royo | Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.  He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.

Malefic Time | Malefic Time is a project created by Luis Royo and Romulo Royo.  It is set in a post apocalyptic world that mixes reality led to the decay of our society with real manifestations of mythological beliefs and superstitions of different cultures.

Rafael Royo-Torres | Dr. Rafael Royo-Torres is a Spanish Paleontologist.  He works in Fundación Conjunto Paleontológico de Teruel-Dinópolis, in Teruel, Spain.  He is one of the discoverers of "Turiasaurus riodevensis", together with Luis Alcalá and Alberto Cobos.

In the Company of Others | In the Company of Others is a stand alone novel written by the Canadian author Julie E. Czerneda.  It was first published by DAW Books in June 2001 and distributed by Penguin Putnam Inc.  In 2002 it won the Prix Aurora Award for Best Long-form work in English.  The cover art of the first edition was created by Luis Royo and Features the main character, Aaron Pardell, sliding along the cables Outside of Thromberg station.

After the War (video game) | After The War is a video game published in 1989 by Dinamic Software, in which the player controls a character through a post-apocalyptic city, probably after a nuclear war.  Released and unreleased artworks by Luis Royo and Alfonso Azpiri reveals that the city should be a post-nuclear version of New York City.
Romulo Royo | Romulo Royo is a contemporary artist, born in 1976 in Zaragoza (Spain).
response.summary_2: The Ice Dragon (the fantasy novella reworked in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo) was written by George R. R. Martin.
